chapters.title: Start With the Job To Be Done

summaryBullets: Understanding demand through the job customers are hiring a product to do creates clearer judgment.
Customer behavior becomes clearer when the underlying struggle is visible.
This matters because innovation improves when products are designed around progress people are trying to make.
A common mistake is segmenting only by demographics while missing the real job.
The pattern becomes easier to see when different customers buy for the same deeper progress even when they look unrelated on the surface.
A practical starting move is to ask what progress the customer seeks, describe the struggle before the product, and segment by job not just by person.
keyTakeaways: Understanding demand through the job customers are hiring a product to do creates clearer judgment. This perspective helps people make clearer decisions and follow through with more discipline when stakes rise.
practice: ask what progress the customer seeks
describe the struggle before the product
segment by job not just by person
review whether the solution actually helps the job

chapters.title: Look for Causal Demand, Not Loose Correlation

summaryBullets: Treating demand as a causal story rather than a statistical pattern alone creates clearer judgment.
Better products come from knowing why a choice happens, not just who made it.
This matters because causal clarity matters because correlation rarely tells you what to build next.
A common mistake is collecting customer data without understanding the moment of purchase.
The pattern becomes easier to see when the dashboard is full while the reason for buying stays blurry.
A practical starting move is to trace the purchase story, identify the push and pull in the choice, and separate cause from correlation.
keyTakeaways: Treating demand as a causal story rather than a statistical pattern alone creates clearer judgment. This perspective helps people make clearer decisions and follow through with more discipline when stakes rise.
practice: trace the purchase story
identify the push and pull in the choice
separate cause from correlation
build the solution around the moment of progress

This matters because adoption improves when designers understand what must be overcome as well as what must be offered.
A common mistake is focusing only on attraction while ignoring anxiety and habit.
The pattern becomes easier to see when the offer looks strong but customers still do not move because the friction stayed active.
A practical starting move is to map the push and the pull, identify the anxieties, and reduce the habit friction.

chapters.title: Design Around the Job, Not Around Features Alone

summaryBullets: Building the offer around the job rather than around a pile of features creates clearer judgment.
Great products simplify the customer's progress instead of adding disconnected capability.
This matters because clarity improves when features are judged by the job they help complete.
A common mistake is adding features that do not improve the actual progress the customer is buying.
The pattern becomes easier to see when the product grows while customer success stays confusing or inconsistent.
A practical starting move is to tie every feature to the job, remove features that do not help progress, and design the full use experience.
keyTakeaways: Building the offer around the job rather than around a pile of features creates clearer judgment. This perspective helps people make clearer decisions and follow through with more discipline when stakes rise.

design the full use experience
